evidence: We intend to shift our workforce toward higher-value roles through talent reskilling and upskilling, redeployment strategies, and driving adoption of artificial intelligence and modern technologies.
We will continue to incur costs related to our deployment of artificial intelligence and compliance with applicable laws and regulations governing data collection, processing and retention.
Although we make significant investments in risk management, technology resiliency and cybersecurity measures for the protection of our systems and data
Our current and potential future uses of such tools (and use by our vendors and agents) may expose us to heightened security and privacy risks as well as operational, legal, and reputational risks.
We intend to shift our workforce toward higher-value roles through talent reskilling and upskilling, redeployment strategies, and driving adoption of artificial intelligence and modern technologies.
we face challenges related to data governance, including the need to manage our data with the aim to meet regulatory requirements and create a foundation for the use of artificial intelligence tools.
Adversaries increasingly use artificial intelligence to develop new hacking tools, exploit vulnerabilities, obscure malicious activities and increase the difficulty of detecting threats.

Data produced by or contained in artificial intelligence tools may contain inaccuracies, and our investments in such technologies and related organizational changes may not deliver the expected benefits, which could result in operational disruptions, inefficiencies, unexpected costs and regulatory disallowances.

Beginning in January 2027, our businesses subject to the California Consumer Privacy Act will also be subject to new regulations related to, among other things, the use of artificial intelligence tools to automate certain decisions.

limitations: No quantified AI-attributable dollar amounts or headcount figures appear in the excerpts; investment intensity scored 3 (named but unquantified).
AI use cases are described at a general level (workforce modernization, data governance, cybersecurity) without deployment status or metrics, limiting concreteness to 3.

Risk disclosure excerpts are from risk_factors and mdna sections and include company-specific mechanisms (regulatory disallowances, California CCPA AI regulations, adversarial AI hacking tools), supporting a score of 4, but no quantified risk exposure or named governance body is cited to reach 5.
